aliases: South Indian egg pancake with cheese
Indian fermented rice pancake egg cheese

english: South Indian egg pancake with cheese
Indian fermented rice pancake egg cheese

food_subgroup: Fermented rice and lentil pancakes with toppings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general uttapam entry (FOODON_03315763); the egg-and-cheese topped variant is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific uttapam code. A0CKP (Pancake) is the closest defensible parent category for a thick fermented rice-lentil pancake.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for uttapam or this specific topped variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium uttappam with egg and cheese topping, approximately 150 g, as typically served at a South Indian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Uttapam (plain, cooked) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; egg (whole, cooked) and processed cheese values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Plain uttapam nutritional base derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) for fermented rice-lentil pancake. Egg contribution estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (whole egg, cooked, scrambled, FDC ID 172185). Processed cheese contribution estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cheese, processed, American, FDC ID 173414). A composite per-100g value was calculated assuming a typical 150g serving comprising approximately 100g uttapam base, 30g egg, and 20g processed cheese, then normalised to per-100g. Values are estimates; direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant item is not available.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of South Indian fermented foods — Idli and Dosa analogues; Uttapam GI estimated from fermented rice-lentil batter analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Gopalpura et al., Asia Pacific Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for egg-cheese uttapam was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 51 based on analogous fermented rice-lentil South Indian foods (idli GI ~46–54, dosa GI ~50–55) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Gopalpura et al., Asia Pacific Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2010). The egg and cheese toppings contribute protein and fat which further moderate postprandial glycaemic response. GL per serving = (51 × 24.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 18.8 g, based on 150g serving size.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from cheese and salt
hypertension.guidance: Uttappam with egg and cheese contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100g (465 mg per 150g serving), primarily from processed cheese and added salt; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation or substitute fresh cheese to lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic index from fermented rice-lentil base, partially offset by protein and fat from egg and cheese
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 51 (low-moderate) and a glycaemic load of approximately 18.8g per 150g serving, this dish has a moderate glycaemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume one serving with a non-starchy vegetable side and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the fermented batter base raises blood glucose more slowly than plain rice but the portion carbohydrate load remains significant.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and cholesterol from egg and processed cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 150g serving provides approximately 4.7g saturated fat and 143mg cholesterol from the egg and processed cheese toppings; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider substituting processed cheese with a lower-fat alternative to reduce saturated fat intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Uttappam with Egg and Cheese is a South Indian savoury pancake made from a fermented batter of ground rice and urad dal (split black lentils), cooked on a griddle and topped with a whole egg and melted processed cheese. The fermentation process, typically lasting 8–12 hours, develops a mildly tangy flavour and a soft, spongy texture with a slightly crisp underside. The egg is cracked directly onto the batter surface while cooking, and cheese is added to melt over the top, creating a rich, protein-enhanced topping. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at South Indian banana-leaf restaurants and Indian-Muslim eateries as a breakfast or brunch item, often accompanied by coconut chutney and sambar. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density of approximately 198 kcal per 100g, with meaningful protein (8.2g/100g) from the egg and cheese, moderate carbohydrate (24.5g/100g) from the fermented rice-lentil base, and notable calcium (112mg/100g) from the cheese. The fermented base contributes dietary fibre (1.4g/100g) and beneficial organic acids. The dish has a moderate glycaemic index (estimated 51) due to the fermentation process and the protein-fat buffering effect of the egg and cheese toppings. Clinically, sodium content (310mg/100g) warrants attention for hypertensive individuals, and saturated fat from cheese and egg is relevant for cardiovascular risk management. This dish is suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarians but is not vegan, gluten-free, or halal-certified in most Singapore restaurant contexts due to egg and dairy content.

confidence_reason: No exact match for egg-cheese uttapam was found in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo databases. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from ICMR-NIN plain uttapam data combined with USDA SR Legacy values for cooked egg and processed cheese, normalised to a typical serving composition. GI is an analogous estimate from fermented rice-lentil South Indian food data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant item is not available.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; direct laboratory analysis of this specific field survey data menu item from Singapore Banana Leaf Restaurant - Circular Road is not available. Values should be treated as indicative. 2. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Dairy and eggs'; this has been corrected to 'Grains and staples' as the primary energy base is the fermented rice-lentil pancake, with egg and cheese as toppings. 3. The cuisine tag in the input was 'Malaysian'; this has been corrected to 'South Indian' as uttapam is a South Indian dish of Tamil origin, though it is widely served in Singapore and Malaysia. 4. GI value of 51 is an analogous estimate based on fermented rice-lentil South Indian foods (idli, dosa) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for egg-cheese uttapam was found. 5. Halal status is marked false as processed cheese and egg sourcing at this specific restaurant cannot be confirmed as halal-certified without direct verification. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not directly reported for this composite dish in any primary source; these values carry higher uncertainty. 7. No -1 sentinel values remain in per_100g; all fields have been estimated from component data. 8. Price data (SGD 6.00) preserved from input source (field survey data) for reference only; not used as a nutritional evidence source.
